ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class; no specific entry for Singaporean Teh O Siu Dai (less sweet variant). Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers black tea as a liquid beverage. No specific code exists for the Singaporean siu dai (less sweet) preparation variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Teh O Siu Dai or Singaporean sweetened black tea variants.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Teh O Siu Dai is approximately 250 ml (250 g), as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Teh O Siu Dai — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Teh O Siu Dai (CR ID D09020073). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Teh O Siu Dai specifically. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on sucrose GI of 65 (University of Sydney GI Database) diluted in a predominantly water-based beverage with 5.1g sugar per 100g. The effective GI of a lightly sweetened beverage is moderated by the low sugar concentration. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (5.1g/100g × 250g = 12.75g) / 100 = 7.0. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.1g/100g) in a sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (4mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Teh O Siu Dai and request Teh O Kosong (unsweetened black tea), as even reduced-sugar sweetened beverages are associated with elevated blood pressure independent of sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.1g added sugar per 100g; liquid sugar causes rapid glycaemic excursion and qualifies as an SSB under ADA guidelines regardless of the 'less sugar' designation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Teh O Siu Dai entirely and substitute with Teh O Kosong (no sugar), as liquid sugar in any quantity causes faster postprandial glucose elevation than equivalent solid-food carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages, even at reduced-sugar levels, is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction; sodium is low (4mg/100g) and fat is absent.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Teh O Siu Dai and prefer Teh O Kosong, as the added sugar (5.1g/100g) contributes to SSB-associated cardiovascular risk even at reduced quantities, while the low sodium and zero fat profile are favourable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.1g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the 'siu dai' reduction is insufficient to clear the RA added-sugar concern despite black tea polyphenols being present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should order Teh O Kosong to benefit from black tea polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh O Siu Dai is a popular Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) beverage consisting of black tea brewed with a reduced quantity of sugar, served hot or iced. 'Teh O' denotes plain black tea without milk (as opposed to 'Teh' which includes evaporated or condensed milk), and 'siu dai' is Cantonese for 'less sweet' or 'less sugar'. It is a lighter, lower-calorie variant of the standard sweetened Teh O. Per 100g, it provides 21 kcal, 5.1g carbohydrate (all from sugar), 0.1g protein, 0g fat, 0.1g dietary fibre, and 4mg sodium. Despite the reduced sugar designation, it still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under clinical guidelines (ADA, AHA), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. Ordering Teh O Kosong (no sugar) is the recommended substitution for health-conscious consumers. Black tea itself contains polyphenols and flavonoids with potential antioxidant properties, but these benefits are partially offset by the added sugar in the siu dai preparation. It is a ubiquitous breakfast and snack beverage across Singapore hawker centres, kopitiams, and food courts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020073), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All per-100g values are exact matches from the HPB entry. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this specific preparation.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D09020073); black tea with minimal sugar contains no fat by composition. iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB — confirmed zero per approved source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived fat. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Teh O Siu Dai preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020073 as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (55, low) based on sucrose GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for low sugar concentration in a water-based beverage; direct GI measurement for Teh O Siu Dai is not available in any approved source.
